full_response:
Let  

• R = r0 r1 … r(n-1) be the inscription that runs clockwise on the third ring (all
characters are readable).  
• P = p0 p1 … p(m-1) be Balbo’s length-m word.  
  Some pi ’s are unreadable; we write the unreadable symbol as ‘?’.  
  By definition ‘?’ matches every σ ∈ Σ.

Because the ring is circular we want to know whether there is a rotation   
k ∈ {0,…, n-1} such that for every j = 0,…,m-1  

    pj = ‘?’   or   pj = r(k+j) mod n .                                (1)

We must obtain the answer within the same asymptotic running time as in part (b)
(nothing slower than 𝑂(n log n)).



--------------------------------------------------------------------
1 Encoding the alphabet
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Give every character of Σ a distinct positive integer code.
Because |Σ| > 2 a single 64–bit integer is plenty; denote the code of
character c by code(c).  
Put code(‘?’)   = 0.

Define two numerical arrays

    a[j] = code(pj)                (j = 0…m-1)  
    b[i] = code(ri)                (i = 0…n-1)

and extend b to length 2n to cope with wrap-around:

    b[n+i] = b[i]  for  0 ≤ i < n .                                    (2)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
2 Masking the readable positions
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let  

    mask[j] = 0    if pj = ‘?’  
               1    otherwise.                                        (3)

(mask tells us which positions of the pattern are actually constrained.)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
3 Reducing the problem to three convolutions
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a fixed rotation k define

    Δk = Σ_{j=0}^{m-1}  mask[j]·(a[j] − b[k+j])².                   (4)

Observation  
Δk = 0  iff  condition (1) holds at rotation k.

Proof.  
If pj = ‘?’ then mask[j]=0 and the j-term in (4) is 0.  
If pj ≠ ‘?’ the j-term is (a[j]−b[k+j])² which is 0 exactly when
a[j]=b[k+j], i.e. pj = r(k+j) mod n.  ∎



Expand (4):

Δk = Σ mask[j]·a[j]²                           (constant)  
     + Σ mask[j]·b[k+j]²                        (depends on k)  
     − 2 Σ a[j]·b[k+j].                                              (5)

• C  :=  Σ mask[j]·a[j]²         is the same for every k.  
• U_k := Σ mask[j]·b[k+j]²      can be obtained by one convolution.  
• V_k := Σ a[j]·b[k+j]          can be obtained by one convolution.

Hence

    Δk  =  C + U_k − 2·V_k.                                          (6)

A rotation k is a match  iff  Δk = 0.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
4 Computing U and V with FFTs
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write rev(X) for an array X reversed left-to-right.

PRECOMPUTATIONS  
C      ← Σ_{j=0}^{m-1} mask[j]·a[j]²

Arrays to be convolved (all are padded with zeros so that their
common length L is the first power of two ≥ 2n+m):

A  ←  rev(a)                     length m  
M  ←  rev(mask)                  length m  
B  ←  b0…b(2n-1)                 length 2n  
B² ←  B element-wise squared     length 2n

CONVOLUTIONS  (using one FFT per array):

V ←  A ★ B        // ★ denotes convolution  
U ←  M ★ B²

(The index k of interest will be k+m−1 in the convolution arrays.)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
5 Whole algorithm
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Algorithm RingMatch(R, P)

1  n ← |R| ;  m ← |P| ;  D ← R‖R                // duplicate ring  
2  encode characters ⇒ arrays a, mask (length m),  
    B,B² (length 2n).  
3  C ← Σ mask[j]·a[j]²  
4  A ← rev(a) ;  M ← rev(mask)  
5  V ← FFT-Convolve(A , B)  
    U ← FFT-Convolve(M , B²)  
6  for k = 0 … n−1 do  
        Δ ← C + U[k+m−1] − 2·V[k+m−1]  
        if Δ = 0 then return “match at rotation k”  
7  return “no match”.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
6 Correctness proof
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We prove:

The algorithm outputs “match at rotation k”  iff  condition (1) holds
for that k.

• By the derivation above Δk of (6) equals the left–hand side of (4).  
• By Observation Δk = 0  iff  (1) is satisfied.  
• Step 6 declares a match exactly when Δk = 0.

Hence the algorithm is correct. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------
7 Complexity analysis
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let L ≤ 4n be the FFT length chosen.

Two forward FFTs and two inverse FFTs per convolution ⇒  
time per convolution   𝑂(L log L).

We perform two convolutions, therefore

    Time   =  𝑂(L log L) = 𝑂(n log n).  
    Space  =  𝑂(L)       = 𝑂(n).

Exactly the same asymptotic bounds as in part (b).



\boxed{Modify the part (b) FFT algorithm as above:  
encode ‘?’ in the pattern as 0, build the three
arrays A=rev(a), M=rev(mask), B (ring doubled) and compute  

Δ_k = C + (M ★ B²)_{k+m-1} − 2·(A ★ B)_{k+m-1}.  

A rotation k is a match iff Δ_k = 0.  
The procedure uses two FFTs, runs in 𝑂(n\log n) time and 𝑂(n) space.}

Confidence: 0.36
